forecasts.reasoning: Although John Morgan is publicly flirting with a 2026 run for Florida governor, an official announcement before July 1, 2025, is highly unlikely. In mid-May 2025, Morgan stated he is "waiting to see how the gubernatorial field shapes up," which will not happen by early July. While there is a June 12, 2025, statutory deadline for him to register under a newly formed third party (to satisfy a 365-day party membership requirement), he could meet this legal threshold by simply registering the party and changing his affiliation without formally announcing a campaign. Furthermore, Morgan has a long history of teasing gubernatorial runs to generate media buzz only to ultimately not run (as seen in the 2018 and 2022 cycles). He can also bypass the June deadline entirely by choosing to run as an independent/No Party Affiliation (NPA) candidate, allowing him to indefinitely prolong his exploratory media tour. With no concrete steps toward an official campaign launch established just weeks before the July 1 cutoff, the likelihood he formally commits is very low.

As of early June 2025, the knowledge cutoff date, there is widespread lamentation by Bulgarian officials regarding the Botas gas agreement, commonly referring to it as severely disadvantageous and pushing Bulgargaz toward bankruptcy. However, Bulgarian Energy Minister Zhecho Stankov and MP Delyan Dobrev have publicly emphasized mere weeks prior (in mid-May 2025) that the contract contains no termination clause, and that unilaterally cancelling it would trigger a penalty of over 4 billion levs (approx. $2.2 billion). Consequently, Bulgaria's strategy has been to seek renegotiation with Turkey and BOTAŞ or plead for a bilateral exit, rather than unilaterally pulling the plug. BOTAŞ has shown no willingness to simply walk away from a highly lucrative take-or-pay contract. With only 25 days remaining between the knowledge cutoff (June 6, 2025) and the resolution date (July 1, 2025), the bureaucratic and diplomatic inertia strongly suggest that a formal cancellation by either party is highly improbable within this tight window. A unilateral repudiation by Bulgaria is theoretically possible but remains very unlikely given the immediate financial ruin and legal penalties it would invite without an agreed alternative.

The knowledge cutoff is June 6, 2025, less than one month before the resolution date of July 1, 2025. Multiple sources from 2024 and early 2025 confirm that Sudan has not ratified the AfCFTA. Crucially, Sudan has been suspended from the African Union following military coups, and since April 2023, the country has been engulfed in a massive, devastating civil war between the SAF and RSF. The absence of a functioning national parliament, the suspension from AU activities, and the absolute focus on a survival-level armed conflict make the complex political and legal process of ratifying a continental trade agreement virtually impossible in this timeframe. There is no news indicating any progress toward ratification or an end to the political crisis. Thus, the probability of ratification happening before July 1, 2025 is effectively zero, so I submit the minimum allowed probability of 5%.
